answer:
**In the corpus, the Old English elegies appear as individual chapters within the single collection *Old English Elegies* (unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e). Several of these poems prominently feature the "man-and-his-lord" (or analogous retainer-lord) relation—a core motif in Anglo-Saxon poetry, often tied to themes of exile (*wræclastas*), loss of the "gold-friend" (*goldwine*) or "treasure-giver" (*māgþegn* or *dǣġeġiefa*), and yearning for the lost hall-life of gift-giving in the mead-hall.**[^1]

The key examples include:

- **The Wanderer** (ch. 11): The speaker, an earth-walker (*eorla weard*), laments the death of his "gold-friend" (*goldwine*) and "beloved lord" (*dryhten*), from whom he received treasures at the feast. He wanders as a "lordless man" (*anhaga*), binding his sorrow inwardly as a noble custom, and dreams of embracing his "liege-lord" (*hīene dryhten*) on the "gift-throne" (*giefstōl*).[^2][^3]

- **The Seafarer** (ch. 5): Even the proudest man, "with a lord so dear" (*læfdon lēofne*), feels anxiety on sea-voyages, forgoing harp, rings, and wife for the waves' compulsion.[^4]

- **Ik þis giedd wræce** (ch. 3, often called *The Wife's Lament*): Though from a woman's perspective, it depicts separation from "my lord" (*min drihten*) who departs over the waves, leaving her a "friendless exile" (*winelēas wræclasta*) seeking service amid kin-feud.[^5]

- **Widsith** (ch. 14): The scop boasts of serving "many lords" (*mānyg drihten*) across tribes, receiving rings and treasures in mead-halls from high-born gift-givers.[^6]

- **Ful oft þæt gegongeþ** (ch. 2, *The Fates of Men*): Alludes to the "lordless exile" (*dryhtlēas wræclasta*), hated and without providers.[^7]

Other chapters in the collection (e.g., *Deor* in ch. 13, *The Ruin* in ch. 16) evoke related themes of transience and lost glory but less directly emphasize the personal lord-retainer bond. No other Old English works in the corpus are classified as elegies.[^8]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), [translation/](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/).

[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), [p-9462cc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).

[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), [p-9cca5e](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9cca5e).

[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 5 (*The Seafarer*), [p-549618](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-549618).

[^5]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 3 (*Ik this giedd wreche*), [p-97aedc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/03-ik-this-giedd-wreche-bi-me-ful-geomorre/translation/#p-97aedc).

[^6]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14 (*Widsith*), [p-530d9f](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-530d9f).

[^7]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 2 (*Ful oft þæt gegongeþ*), [p-3de313](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/02-ful-oft-thaet-gegongeth-mid-godes-meahtum/translation/#p-3de313).

[^8]: *Old English Elegies*, full collection overview via [list_chapters](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e).

args.query: "my lord" OR "his lord" OR "the lord" exile OR wanderer
"gold-friend" OR "treasure-giver" OR "liege-lord" OR "ring-giver" OR "gold-lord" old english
